[2026-07-04 22:44:21 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED triage team' action='activate the early treatment pathway and perform initial assessment for suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke' objects='patient, symptom onset time, vital signs, medication history' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose at presentation' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='blood glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='blood glucose does not indicate hypoglycemia' id='branch3'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue glucose management assessment' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='repeat blood glucose sample' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch5'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='document that repeat blood glucose measurement is not required' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity6'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch6'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate body temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature measurements' id='activity7'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='recognize elevated temperature and begin fever management pathway' objects='temperature result' id='activity8'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch8'>
              <activity role='ED team' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='cooling measures' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch9'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection workup' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch10'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch11'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity12'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='branch12'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature record' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch13'>
      <activity role='ED physician' action='assess TIA or minor stroke presentation, high-risk features, fluctuating or crescendo symptoms, current antiplatelet use, and swallowing status' objects='neurologic findings, medication history, swallowing screen' id='activity14'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='TIA with fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='evaluate patient in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='admission assessment' id='activity15'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='no fluctuating or crescendo TIA symptoms requiring ED admission consideration' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='continue risk-based management planning' objects='clinical assessment' id='activity16'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke and medical attention sought within 48 hours of symptom onset' id='branch16'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='start the urgent high-risk TIA or minor stroke management pathway' objects='risk classification, symptom onset time' id='activity17'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
            <branch condition='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is accessible without risky delay' id='branch17'>
              <activity role='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic team' action='perform brain CT scan and initiate antiplatelet therapy as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan, antiplatelet therapy' id='activity18'/>
              <activity role='stroke-prevention team' action='provide other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatments' id='activity19'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='delay accessing specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='perform evaluation with brain CT scan and initiate antiplatelet therapy as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan, antiplatelet therapy' id='activity20'/>
              <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='inpatient stroke-prevention plan' id='activity21'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke and medical attention sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch19'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='urgent referral' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch20'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='perform brain CT scan and initiate antiplatelet therapy as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT scan, antiplatelet therapy' id='activity23'/>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity24'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='clinician' action='confirm that brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage before immediate antiplatelet initiation when indicated' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity25'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
        <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch21'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='prepare immediate antiplatelet initiation' objects='approved antiplatelet options' id='activity26'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
            <branch condition='choose ASA regimen' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='select ASA-based antiplatelet treatment' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity27'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
                <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral ASA' id='branch23'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA with a 160 mg loading dose immediately, followed by enteric coated ASA 81-325 mg daily and usually 81 mg/day maintenance' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity28'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient can take oral ASA' id='branch24'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='start ASA with a 160 mg loading dose immediately, followed by enteric coated ASA 81-325 mg daily and usually 81 mg/day maintenance' objects='oral ASA' id='activity29'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='choose clopidogrel regimen' id='branch25'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='start clopidogrel with a 300 mg loading dose immediately followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel' id='activity30'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='choose extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen' id='branch26'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='select extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA treatment' objects='extended-release dipyridamole, ASA' id='activity31'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
                <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral ASA loading dose' id='branch27'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA 160 mg loading dose, then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg twice daily when feasible' objects='rectal or gastric ASA, extended-release dipyridamole/ASA' id='activity32'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient can take oral ASA loading dose' id='branch28'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='load with ASA 160 mg immediately, then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='oral ASA, extended-release dipyridamole/ASA' id='activity33'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch29'>
          <activity role='stroke-prevention team' action='continue or adjust tailored stroke-prevention treatment without immediate new antiplatelet initiation' objects='existing therapy plan' id='activity34'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='stroke-prevention team' action='provide appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies whether the patient is admitted to hospital or discharged' objects='Modules 5 and 10 assessments and therapies' id='activity35'/>
</process>
Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note:
1) Only expand important activities, not all of them.
2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities.
3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities.
4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship.
5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities.
Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them:
1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash' 
   activity relations: exclusive
2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'
   activity relations: sequential
3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials' 
   activity relations: parallel
After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else.

[2026-07-04 22:45:23 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Team leader on : ProcessGeneration, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..

[2026-07-04 22:45:23 INFO] System: **[chatting]**

| Parameter | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

| **phase_prompt** | task: {task} process text: {refinement_process_text} Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note: 1) Only expand important activities, not all of them. 2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities. 3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities. 4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship. 5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities. Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them: 1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash'     activity relations: exclusive 2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'    activity relations: sequential 3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:    sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials'     activity relations: parallel After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text: {format} First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else. |

and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, | **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

   \n            \n              \n                \n                  \n                \n                \n                  \n                \n              \n              \n            \n                  \n                        \n                        \n                          \n                        \n                      \n                    \n                    \n                      \n                    \n                    \n                      \n                      \n                        \n                          \n                        \n                        \n                          \n                        \n                      \n                    \n                  \n                \n                \n                  \n                \n              \n            \n          \n        \n      \n    \n  \n  \n", 'format': "\nWhen the process starts, please output ''\nAn activity is represented as follows:\n\nThe exclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe parallel gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nThe inclusive gateway is represented as follows:\n\n  \n    \n  \n  \n    \n  \n\nAt the end of the process, please output ''\n\nThere are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:\n    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.\n    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.\n    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.\n    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.\n    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.\n    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.\n    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.\n    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.\n"} |

[2026-07-04 22:45:23 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='triage the patient using a standardized priority protocol for suspected TIA or minor stroke' objects='patient with suspected TIA or minor stroke' id='act1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='pg1'>
    <branch id='br1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose sample' id='act2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exg1'>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia present' id='br2'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia' id='act3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='br3'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose sample' id='act4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L and no hypoglycemia' id='br4'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='document blood glucose result and continue standard care' objects='blood glucose result' id='act5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='br5'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature measurements' id='act6'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exg2'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='br6'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='elevated temperature' id='act7'/>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='possible infection source' id='act8'/>
          <inclusiveGateway id='ig1'>
            <branch condition='antipyretic therapy required' id='br7'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='act9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy required' id='br8'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='act10'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='br9'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature measurements' id='act11'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='br10'>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='assess whether the presentation is high-risk TIA, TIA without high-risk features, or minor nondisabling stroke and assess time since symptom onset, current antiplatelet use, contraindications, and swallowing status' objects='clinical history, examination findings, and screening results' id='act12'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exg3'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features' id='br11'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='act13'/>
          <parallelGateway id='pg2'>
            <branch id='br12'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='determine the most appropriate location of care and need for admission' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act59'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exg4'>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='br13'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='refer the patient for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='referral request' id='act14'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours of symptom onset and a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is immediately accessible' id='br14'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='provide immediate care at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act15'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours of symptom onset and delay to a specialized clinic poses risk to patient health' id='br15'>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='evaluate the patient at an appropriately resourced ED' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act16'/>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision' id='act17'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exg5'>
                <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty present' id='br16'>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='evaluate the patient in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act18'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo symptoms not present' id='br17'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='continue urgent outpatient stroke-prevention management' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act19'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch id='br18'>
              <activity role='stroke physician' action='review brain imaging to exclude intracranial hemorrhage' objects='brain CT result' id='act20'/>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention plan' id='act21'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exg6'>
                <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='br19'>
                  <activity role='stroke physician' action='select an immediate antiplatelet regimen' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='act60'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exg7'>
                    <branch condition='ASA regimen selected' id='br20'>
                      <activity role='stroke physician' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose immediately after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage, then continue enteric-coated ASA 81-325 mg daily; most patients should receive 81 mg/day' objects='ASA regimen' id='act22'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exg8'>
                        <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral ASA' id='br21'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='act23'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening and can take oral ASA' id='br22'>
                          <activity role='ED nurse' action='administer oral ASA' objects='oral ASA' id='act24'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen selected' id='br23'>
                      <activity role='stroke physician' action='start clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose immediately after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage, then continue 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='act25'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole/ASA regimen selected' id='br24'>
                      <activity role='stroke physician' action='load with ASA 160 mg first, then start extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid immediately after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage' objects='extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='act26'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exg9'>
                        <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral ASA' id='br25'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='act27'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening and can take oral ASA' id='br26'>
                          <activity role='ED nurse' action='administer oral ASA loading dose' objects='oral ASA loading dose' id='act28'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='br27'>
                  <activity role='stroke physician' action='document existing antiplatelet therapy or contraindication and continue tailored stroke-prevention treatment' objects='medication history or contraindication record' id='act29'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='minor (nondisabling) stroke' id='br28'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='act30'/>
          <parallelGateway id='pg3'>
            <branch id='br29'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='determine the most appropriate location of care and need for admission' objects='minor stroke patient' id='act61'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exg10'>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='br30'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='refer the patient for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services within 24 hours of initial presentation' objects='referral request' id='act31'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours of symptom onset and a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is immediately accessible' id='br31'>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='provide immediate care at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic' objects='minor stroke patient' id='act32'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours of symptom onset and delay to a specialized clinic poses risk to patient health' id='br32'>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='evaluate the patient at an appropriately resourced ED' objects='minor stroke patient' id='act33'/>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='admission decision' id='act34'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch id='br33'>
              <activity role='stroke physician' action='review brain imaging to exclude intracranial hemorrhage' objects='brain CT result' id='act35'/>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to the patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention plan' id='act36'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exg11'>
                <branch condition='patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='br34'>
                  <activity role='stroke physician' action='select an immediate antiplatelet regimen' objects='minor stroke patient' id='act62'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exg12'>
                    <branch condition='ASA regimen selected' id='br35'>
                      <activity role='stroke physician' action='start ASA 160 mg loading dose immediately after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage, then continue enteric-coated ASA 81-325 mg daily; most patients should receive 81 mg/day' objects='ASA regimen' id='act37'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exg13'>
                        <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral ASA' id='br36'>
                          <activity role='ED team' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='act38'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening and can take oral ASA' id='br37'>
                          <activity role='ED nurse' action='administer oral ASA' objects='oral ASA' id='act39'/>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>

                </branch>
                <branch condition='patient is already on an antiplatelet agent or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='br56'>
                  <activity role='stroke physician' action='document existing antiplatelet therapy or contraindication and continue outpatient stroke-prevention treatment' objects='medication history or contraindication record' id='act57'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='stroke team' action='provide appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies from Modules 5 and 10 to all patients whether admitted or discharged' objects='secondary prevention assessment and therapy plan' id='act58'/>
</process>
Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note:
1) Only expand important activities, not all of them.
2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities.
3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities.
4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship.
5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities.
Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them:
1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash' 
   activity relations: exclusive
2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'
   activity relations: sequential
3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials' 
   activity relations: parallel
After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else.
